content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. I want to cry like a newborn baby. Which movie will get me there?

**Score:** 138
**Body:** I don't really care about the genre. I'm just looking for something with a twist or emotional punch that really cuts deep and leaves me completely devastated.

I'm talking about the kind of movie that sticks with you for days and has you ugly crying by the end. What are the saddest, most soul-crushing movies you've ever seen?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Terms of endearment 
2. I can’t believe no one has said My Girl  
3. Dancer in the Dark
4. Sophie's Choice.  Years ago I recommended this movie to young goofy guy I worked with who said "Oooh, I really love sad movies! What's a sad movie I should rent?" (Blockbuster days)  When I asked him how it was the next time I saw him, he looked at me, stricken, and said, "I didn't mean THAT sad!"
5. What Dreams May Come. Husband and I saw it in the theater and we were both ugly crying.
6. Bridge to terabithia
7. Big Fish
8. The Green Mile
9. Beaches.

Steel Magnolias.

What Dreams May Come.

Dear Zachary.

Cramer Vs. Cramer.

Grave Of Fireflies.

Ghost.

Life is Beautiful.
  - I’m a 43 yr old man from the south who rarely cries and Steel Magnolias makes me cry every single time. 

## 2. Recommend a movie to watch when I feel down

**Score:** 44
**Body:** Cannot nail down the type of film I am looking for. But recently, I watched Columbus and The Secret Life of Walter Mitty. they made me feel a little bit better. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Amelie
2. Little Miss Sunshine
3. Airplane! (1980)
4. Stranger Than Fiction 
5. That Thing You Do!

It's just packed with exuberance and optimism and humor.
6. Little Forest (2018 Korean version, Tubi).
7. Napoleon Dynamite 
8. Employee of the Month

Stardust

Running Scared (1986)
9. Based on the two you mentioned I’d suggest these:

* Amélie (2001)

* Lost in Translation (2003)

* The Way Way Back (2013)

* Chef (2014)

* Patterson (2016)
10. What Dreams May Come

## 3. Movies to distract myself from cheating ex?

**Score:** 15
**Body:** I don't even know what kind of movies I'm looking for. I just want to distract my mind. Maybe something semi-optimistic. Nothing that ends with a romantic happily-ever-after angle. I don't know, even something horror or gory might work at this point. Or maybe something very weird that gets my mind away from all the overthinking for a while. I'm even open to animation. Just something as far away from romance as possible. 

  
For a bit of context, I found out a few months ago that my now-ex had been cheating on me for 8 months, and I've been depressed since. What sucks is that it was actually a really healthy relationship from my perspective. My therapist literally went from, "so he was really not a nice person," in the first appointment after learning the basics of what had happened, to "so maybe we need to reframe our mindset as it was too good to be true. Real life is never that great," by the third appointment after deeply discussing the relationship dynamics and how well he used to treat me. So yeah. 

It's his birthday tomorrow. In a few hours, actually. And this will be the first time in 4 years where I can't wish him at 12am, so I need some serious distraction. 

  
Sorry for the TMI, but my head is just all over the place right now. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Watch ted lasso but it is a show
  - Agreed
2. A good fun action movie.

Rush Hour if you want more comedy.

John Wick if you want more violence. 
3. So sorry for you are going through. Just got divorced a year ago (no infidelity) just grew apart,  but it sucks thinking about the past (good times and bad times). I would recommend High Fidelity is really good break-up movie. Hang in there. Things do get better.
4. Withnail and I

First Cow

The Humans

Wendy and Lucy

2001: A Space Odyssey
5. Have you seen "What About Bob?" Things could be worse- you could have Tourettes! 😝 ((I'm very sorry about your situation, BTW. That sucks. When you're an honest and decent person, it's natural to assume others are too and, sadly, users exploit that. My husband and I both were with some rotten people previously, and it's only made us appreciate each other more, so there is that. 🥲) 
6. The Terrifier movies are about as from romantic as humanly possible. You will be so mortified, that you won’t be able to think about anything but bleaching your eyeballs.
7. Check out Tokyo Sonata. The first half is a little wtf am I watching, but the second half is absolutely amazing.
8. Nice guys. A comedic masterpiece without any love interest stuff. 
9. Atomic Blonde (2017) is an action thriller with a strong, kick ass female protagonist. It also happens to have an awesome soundtrack. I watched this when I was having a depressive episode. It kept me entertained. 

## 4. Movies set in freezing temps

**Score:** 15
**Body:** When it hits triple digits, I like to cool down with movies set in freezing weather, like The Thing, The Day After Tomorrow, Whiteout, Wind River, etc. Even better when set in the arctic/antarctic.

&#x200B;

Preferrably the whole/majority of the story takes place in the cold, not just a few scenes. Any genre is fine.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Fargo
  - Fargo is great.  Also the tv show Fargo is pretty solid.  The Terror is a great show too, although it’s not a movie.
2. Frozen (2010)
3. The Thing. The original. 
4. Snowpiercer. Bonus there’s a movie (2013) and a tv series (2020, four seasons) 
  - Did not know there was a series! Thanks.
5. The Heroes of Telemark (1965)

Dead Snow (2009)
  - Love Kirk Douglas. I will have to check it out.

Nazi zombies looks fun, too!
6. Atanarjuat: The Fast Runner (2001) The telling of an Inuit legend of an evil spirit causing strife in the community and one warrior's endurance and battle of its menace. (The best Canadian movie ever made, by a considerable margin).
7. Dead of Winter

## 5. Cosy American rural/semi-rural/suburban movies

**Score:** 13
**Body:** I’m a 34-year-old gay guy living in London who’s only ever lived in the UK. However I’ve always found low stakes films with gentle scores set in rural or suburban (especially 90s) USA to be a weird form of comfort to me, despite my lack of exposure to this in real life. I’m talking The Horse Whisperer, Fried Green Tomatoes, Hocus Pocus, Jumanji, The Perfect Storm, Brokeback Mountain, Sweet Home Alabama… that kind of vibe - American in movie form. Any suggestions would be much appreciated!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Straight Story 1999

Janet Planet 2023

Waiting for Guffman 1996

Edit: A League of Their Own 1992 is set in the 40s
2. A River Runs Through It 

Legends Of The Fall
  - Legends of the Fall is such a good movie!!
3. Field of Dreams
4. The Sandlot 

A Christmas Story

Stand By Me
5. Doc Hollywood (1991)
6. Field of dreams
7. O Brother, Where Art Thou? (2000)

Raising Arizona (1987)

The Lady Killers (2004)

Smokey and the Bandit (1977)

Road House (1989)

Sling Blade (1996)

Fargo (1996)

Kalifornia (1993)
8. Lars and the Real Girl
9. Fly Away Home. Napoleon Dynamite. Bottle Rocket.

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Harrison Ford and Steven Spielberg ‘Were Not 100% on Board’ With ‘Crystal Skull’ and Fought George Lucas Over Adding Aliens

**Score:** 520

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Shoulda fought harder
  - They fought it for like 15 years before finally relenting. In the end they just wanted to do another film together.

It would be nice to see Ford reunite with Spielberg one last time for something that’s not Indy, though. In a parallel universe he did Jurassic Park or Schindler’s List, or his cameo in E.T. made the final cut. 
    - TIL that the role of Oskar Schindler was offered to Harrison Ford.

To be fair, Liam Neeson brought a unique kind of playboy pomp that, to my mind, made the performance that much more memorable.
  - A lot harder.
  - They should have fought him over a lot of stuff in crystal skull. The aliens were not the problem at all. Pretty much every scene with Marion in the jungle was abysmal. 
2. There is the bus. Jump under. I hate this excuse.  They still were involved. Plus the Aliens were not the thing that made the film bad. It was small moments like CGI monkey vining.
  - THIS. Him swinging with the monkeys was 10x more egregious than any of the alien stuff.
3. I didn't have a problem with the aliens.  It was no more absurd than the religious stuff in the other films.  What I had a problem with was all the other stupid stuff, like Shia LeBeouf swinging through the trees like a monkey.
  - I was even fine with the fridge, but I wanted to turn off the movie when that monkey scene happened. 
4. I never got the hate. So in universe, magic to rip a man’s heart out exists, definitive proof of God and his vengeance exists, but aliens is a step too far?

## 2. What is a 10/10 rain scene?

**Score:** 500

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|IHiGS6HMtXeBq)
2. T-Rex breaks out the paddock

3. ![gif](giphy|mXONAwCqYujni)

## 3. Rewatched jurassic world and I still don't know how Claire is faster than a t-rex

**Score:** 171
**Body:** In jurassic park, we saw that Rexy was as fast as a jeep (well, i don't really know if it was a jeep but it was a fast vehicle after all) and yes, this IS Rexy, the exact same t-rex we saw in the first movie. Maybe Claire's heels are actually sonic's shoes in the shape of heels but who knows!

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. If the t-rex is anything like me, he was deliberately going slow to get a better view of that cake.
  - ![gif](giphy|gictytW9IIIkNGIMcs)
  - She got the G Wagon propelling her 
  - Bryce absolutely has a motor in the back of her Honda
2. Her high heels had little rockets in the back
  - ![gif](giphy|26n6Pc14z5DcxvMic)
3. ![gif](giphy|shignv8uvl1Q65jP3s|downsized)

She's got powerful legs
4. The Rex is old by JW, right? Like just number of years-wise, it seems like it's probably not a young, spry member of its species. And it took a little damage from the raptors in JP, so maybe it has arthritis or lingering muscle damage. Just enough to slow it down
5. Those powerful glutes.
6. ![gif](giphy|iXooEoi1FTee6w8FP9)

## 4. Jeffrey Dean Morgan has an impressive range, effortlessly switching between heroes, villains, and morally grey characters. Which role of his is your favorite?

**Score:** 67

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. He was also great as Clay in The Losers.

https://preview.redd.it/pb4xsl155p7h1.jpeg?width=1362&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=12dbc0ba1d80a3c0077f47382c6b53550dc36100
  - Such a great movie. The whole Skippy the IT guy scene is my fave. 
2. I’ve only seen him in The Walking Dead, The Watchmen, and The Boys. It’s pretty much the same character. 
3. Daddy Winchester forever
4. The Comedian. I will always see him as The Comedian. 
5. Negan for sure. I liked him in the movie Possession, too.
6. P.S. I love you is my favorite 
7. He has the range of Axel Rose but not 90's Axel Rose, more like 2026 Axel.
8. The Comedian.  He was great in that role 
9. Loved him in Dune 😂 

Those that know, know.   


https://preview.redd.it/bvugccycap7h1.jpeg?width=588&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=a0007cbadd5ee7058fa00f999a36b4ba61bf42c8

## 5. Catwoman (2004) Holy Hell

**Score:** 65
**Body:** The early 2000s is one of my favorite eras of film and a big part of that reason are the countless strange, sometimes successful, comic book adaptions. For every Spider-Man you had Ghost Rider. For every X-Men you had Daredevil.  
Even the bad ones hold a special place for me. Sure they were bad, but they were also never boring (I even include X-Men: The Last Stand in this argument!).  
A product of their time, leather and heart. They have become nostalgic and I am aware of my rose tinted glasses.

In comes Catwoman. A film I didn’t like then but have grown a new found dislike. “Bad films” tend to receive a reexamining around the 2 decade mark and sometimes we find out they were never really bad. Maybe misunderstood or too far ahead of its time.  
Catwoman was bad then and it is very bad now. For a film that focuses hard on sexuality and the beauty business, it ends up saying absolutely nothing at all.  
Character motives are childish, the action scenes have no gravity or weight, and the script is a perfect example of “and *then* this happens” as opposed to “this happens *because* of xyz.”  
Scenes meander with strange and laughable acting. Halle Berry sniffing cat nip and meowing like a cat is as absurd as it sounds.  
The passage of time is also nonexistent with things happening either offscreen or via a voiceover giving no complexity to the drama at hand.

In the end, what’s most devastating is the lost opportunity of Halle Berry playing a legitimate Selina Kyle/Catwoman. She’s a great actress and the looks to play a convincing anti-hero.  
Selina Kyle (named Patience Phillips in this movie for some strange reason) can be a deeply complex person to follow and it could’ve been an interesting character to dig into; even if they decided to do the beauty storyline this film was trying to critique on.

1/2 star added strictly for Halle. Everything else is truly is as bad they say.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The fucking basketball scene is unwatchable.
  - You ever wonder if that shit is why Benjamin Bratt was playing basketball in his first scene in Doctor Strange?
  - What about the see-saw scene in Daredevil 
    - Look. They're both fucking awful. But geeeezuz..at least one movie had the excuse of having a person pretending to be blind 😂
      - I unironically love Daredevil. Colin Ferrel is insane in that flick lmao
2. I cannot remember if I ever actually watched this, but I do remember that Halle Berry accepted her Razzie for the film *in person*, which made me appreciate her ability to laugh at herself (and her willingness to admit the film was ass.) 
  - Yes, she did accept it! 

What makes it better is she had her Oscar in another hand lol
3. It’s better on mute 
  - Only if you are blind.
4. Meh. 15 year old me liked it. 🤷

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. I'm not sure I've seen a more perfectly constructed film than Rosemary's Baby

**Score:** 126
**Body:** When I say this, there are many, many films that are more artfully, more cinematically, more elevatedly, or even creatively constructed. In many you can just feel the director's auteur-ness showing in completely admirable ways, or alternately, in very satisfying genre-guided ways, illumining convention spectacularly, but, in rewatching Rosemary's Baby for the umpteenth time last night we were absolutely struck by how there is zero fat on this film. Every scene, every shot, every performance tone and note seem to work in a completely tireless movie that spends the right amount of time and emphasis required, beat by beat. And Mia Farrow's voice performance floats through all this leanness almost operatically with tremulous excitement, anxiety and terror. The film is like a clockwork. Total appreciation. There is a kind of ease with which it moves, that does not call attention to itself.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I’ve long felt the same about Rosemary’s Baby, as well.   Its pacing. Its sense of focus and, as you say, tautness.

Producer Bob Evans, the patron of Polanski on this film, declared it a masterpiece.  Evans expressly said, regarding Chinatown, that he did not *know* whether that latter film was a masterpiece.  I’m with perhaps the minority that agree with Evans.  I admire Rosemary’s
 Baby for the same reason why, purely structurally, I rate Goodfellas
 higher than Casino; A Clockwork Orange (“a nearly perfect film”— Vincent Canby) higher than The Shining; the original Godfather (the Oedipus Rex of movies) higher than Godfather II.
  - Adding on here, reflecting on your comment some more, as I mention in another comment, this sense of tautness I think has to do with how one feels in rewatch, especially multiple rewatches. Films have have a certain "looseness" in how they are made, that is filled with the anticipation of the unknown in the first watch. I can watch Omar Sharif grow from a speck on the horizon with unique tension if I don't know the shot - which as famous and amazing as they come. But, when I watch it again, and again...and then again, my thoughts are filled with other perceptions. I'm admiring the shot. I'm appreciating it. There is lag, and I'm filling it. It doesn't mean that it is inferior to a shot that I don't have to fill, but some films just aren't like that. They films work like clockwork even on the 10th watch, scene to scene, line to line.

Some of this is probably very idiosyncratic from viewer to viewer, because it matters how emotionally invested I am in a character or film moment, which probably helps determine if its lagging or not in the 5th watch. I can watch Leave Her to Heaven and its just clockwork. Someone else would be like: Ok, enjoyable film. So I'm not fully sure. 

Rosemary's Baby, my connections to the characters and events are so brimming, every space if filled with attention for me, on rewatch. Some of that is made possible though by the film's structure and direction, I'd have to imagine. Some of it, in other films, is accomplished by genre and style (for instance many classic Noir films have a clockwork feeling about them when you rewatch over and over, because the aesthetic holds so enjoyably in the very good ones). Rosemary's Baby is interesting in that it really doesn't seem to partake in genre cliche's much for its structure, but feels self-generating in its leanness, out of Polanski's sensibilities.

I instinctively feel though, in your list of film comparisons, that you are also talking about something more than I mean, but which could be quite connected. 
  - Love what you are saying here, and it good to get some agreement and even clarification on what I was feeling. It's just a very hard to describe tautness...without rushing at all. 
2. My favorite thing about the movie is that there is no specific"twist" moment where everything changes and she suddenly realizes she's in danger. It is a completely smooth gradual burn, nothing obvious, little by little, piece by piece, until she's in the phone booth making the call and she says what we've all been feeling intuitively for the whole movie. 

A lesser film would have had a scene where the neighbors turn obviously bad or Cassavetes becomes evil or something and that's when Rosemary learns what's up. But I love that the film lets you just feel the creeping dread with her, not really understanding what's happening or why, but instinctively knowing it's dangerous. 
  - >My favorite thing about the movie is that there is no specific"twist" moment where everything changes and she suddenly realizes she's in danger.

And the way it is done in waves. She has concerns, intuitions, then they are assuaged. Then bigger concerns, and then assuaged. Up and down it goes in increasing amplitude. It's such a psychologically rhythmical film. 
3. Rosemary's Baby is a very good movie, but I read the book after watching it, and I gotta say, even a movie that good isn't better than the book. They are 98% similar, but that 2% is what makes the book better. Been a while since I read or watched, but my problem with the movie was it was difficult to tell how much time had passed, which the book makes explicit and makes some scenes make more sense. Also, when Rosemary is impregnated, the film is a bit too vague and dream-like to know what's going on, when the book is obvious that she's going through the secret door in the closet.

Also, it is wild to me that Rosemary's Baby, The Stepford Wives, and Sliver were all based on books by the same author.
  - I feel the same way regarding IT and Shawshank Redemption
4. Rosemary's Baby was great, though I am not sure about the perfectly constructed part or how to really analyze that. Spending time on characters, atmosphere etc can always seem important to some or redundant to others. I am not sure if RB is in any way more perfect about what it emphasizes than other good movies, what exactly do you mean by that.

Although I think it is one of the greatest horrors, I watched the other 2 movies from Polansky's apartment trilogy, Repulsion and Tenant, and thought they were just as strong, I even prefered them slightly more. 

Tenant is a bit messier in terms of plot but has something really special about it and the anxiety it builds, it's maybe even my favorite. Repulsion is maybe the most perfect psychological surreal trip. I keep changing my mind between which of these two I like more. 

I can understand why Rosemary is the most popular, because it has the most tangible plot, but all three are genius horrors.
  - >I am not sure if RB is in any way more perfect about what it emphasizes than other good movies

Yeah, all debatable. Just relating the overwhelming sense I got on rewatch for the umpteenth time. That's why I didn't assert that it **was** the most perfectly constructed, only that I'm not sure that I've seen a **more** perfectly constructed film, followed by all sorts of qualifying language. There aren't many films that I've rewatched many times and the first major reaction I had was "wow, that really is so incredibly lean". If I had to qualify it, I think the feeling came from knowing what scene is coming, what dialogue is coming, and there just being absolutely no "lag" in my experience of it, no drift of my attention or focus. I'm absolutely engaged frame by frame. A lot of times what keeps you locked in with a film is not knowing what is going to happen, and even with absolutely great films there can be a kind of attention lag in scenes because of rewatch foreknowledge - this had none of that for me this time. But, there are lots and lots and lots of films I prefer to Rosemary's Baby. It might not be in my top 100 preference films (but probably makes it).

I'm relating a real time reaction to just how incredibly lean it felt this time. It's just cinema enthusiasm. People don't really have to agree with it at all. If someone said it was a "meh" horror film or only Polanski's 7th best film, I wouldn't mind.
5. It's funny that this label of perfect construction is usually pinned to another Polanski - Chinatown. 

Not much to say, RB is indeed very well done, but I prefer The Tenant as an overall experience. It has more flaws and rugged edges, but I think that makes for a more compelling and disturbing experience. 

## 2. Barry Lyndon, The Sublime and Leo Tolstoy

**Score:** 27
**Body:** After revisiting Barry Lyndon for the first time in years, my mind was racing throughout about the exploration of the sublime in this Kubrick classic and why Kubrick was clearly making direct throwbacks to the Sublime/Romantic movement in literature. 

I think something that crystallised my viewing of this is from a famous passage in Leo Tolstoy’s War and Peace from one of its central characters. I won’t actually quote from the book, but the gist of the scene is that a character who has largely been governed by his own vanities finally gets a glimpse of the sublime nature of existence and realises that he has been completely blinded to this due to his petty grievances.

This really got me thinking about the constant juxtaposition throughout Barry Lyndon between the incredible shots of the natural world and the manner in which the characters make absolute no reference to such beauty. I think, in a similar vain to Tolstoy, that Kubrick was using this juxtaposition to show a world that was so caught up in its own pettiness and pride, fuelled largely by an honour culture where self-image is everything, that they fail to see the external world because of their own grievances. The sublime surrounds them and yet they are completely blind to it. 

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Good or bad, handsome or ugly, rich or poor, they are all equal now.

Two scenes spring to mind in reading your post:

The Highway robbery, I feel it shows especially well the absurdity of the machinations of man. They are both essentially playing characters in the interaction, while the sublime beauty of the forest surrounds them.

Barrys encountering the German lady, in which he does perceive the sublime, which not coincidentally involves him acting outside of his role as a British soldier.

## 3. The Hunt proves that rumors spread and destroy faster than wildfires.

**Score:** 16
**Body:** Got a question for you: What is worse? Being ostracized for something you actually did, or being hunted for something you cannot even imagine doing?

The Hunt (Jagten) explores the latter, and it is an uncompromising, agonizing watch. It is terrifying exactly because it looks so utterly mundane. As a teacher myself, the sheer speed at which Lucas's life is dismantled hit me on a deeply visceral level. It disturbed me a lot.

Thomas Vinterberg built this film with brutal simplicity. There is no complex camera work, no massive set builds, no extravagant costumes, and no rocket science. Just a camera, well lit scenes, and human emotion pushed right up to the lens. It plays out like an everyday documentary of a man's execution.

The way he stripped the movie of a soundtrack and kept it minimal makes the dread literally crawl into your nerves. Now I know why for years, cinephiles and critics keep choosing to dissect the actual psychology of the film rather than its technical elements.

The movie perfectly establishes the stable, quiet rhythm of Lucas's life, and then forces the audience to deteriorate right alongside him. An ill word is spoken, and without a single shred of actual investigation, the community tears him apart. You would not believe how the climax gets bloodier and more disturbing purely through the psychological violence of a terrified mob.

From a screenwriting standpoint, the script's absolute brilliance lies in what it refuses to give the audience. It never resorts to cheap melodrama, no tearful apologies, no warm hugs of redemption, and absolutely zero true catharsis. That denial is exactly what makes the film so heavy and grounded. 

Mads Mikkelsen anchors the entire blueprint with a masterclass in subtle, suffocated acting. He owns every single frame he is in.

And the ending is the perfect, chilling final bolt in the engine. It leaves us on a highly ambiguous, terrifying note, the gunshot in the woods proving that even if the law clears your name, the bullet of public perception will always be aiming for the back of your head.

I guess it is a rightly revered psychological masterpiece, and quite possibly one of the best movies ever made.

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Excellent write up. I particularly agree with your comment about how it offers virtually no catharsis. If you haven't seen it already, there's an alternate ending where >!Lucas is murdered at the end !<that is even more nihilistic than the actual ending.

Also a random aside, I live in a town with a man who was unfortunately a victim of the satanic panic in the 1980s. It completely undid him and ruined his life. An even more disturbing contrast is that the lives of the people who accused him, and the parents, and the police and everyone else, just kind of... went on. It's a sobering thought that one tiny misunderstood comment could completely unravel and destroy so much, so quickly.

I see the same desperation in Mads Mikkelsen's eyes as I do in him. It's like an emptiness that occasionally has flashes of the person that he used to be. Really haunting shit, and Mikkelsen does it like it's nothing.
  - You're right to point that alternate ending out. Probably would have been the wrong choice since we all wanted Lucas to have justice and peace. Glad they didn't go with it, but still left us a more unsettling ending.
2. I love this movie (and *Druk* — Vinterberg directing Mikkelsen is a great combo), but your write-up screams of heavy LLM usage. The praise of your write-up I see in the other comments is causing me to further question the authenticity of the discussion here.

That being said, I think the implicit aspects of the film are the most powerful, like the kid internalizing what they were shown in the video and callously using that against the teacher. Feels very human. What happens inside the head of the kid during the line drawn between those two plot points is pretty much totally unspoken, allowing the viewer to project their own internal self onto the character. Almost feels like a horror movie trick when a particularly gruesome part is left to the viewer's imagination, except here's it's completely social/psychological.
  - My own draft was rough, like real rough. So I polished it with LLM. After all you can't just rely on a machine to review a movie. That will not be fair.

That being said, your insight about the imaginations in the movie are spot on. It's really disturbing when a script provides blanks and your mind fills them in with harrowing details and connects them. I guess that would instantly connect you with the person shown on the screen and you would feel his emotions coming through, right away.
    - Personally, I'll take rough and real over machine-polished any day. Going deep into what a film *is* instead of tallying the things it *isn't* is a useful device for avoiding the tropes of computer-assisted writing. Don't want to speak for anyone else, but I'm sure others feel similarly.

Your post got me to look up Vinterberg again and see that he has a series out from 2024 that I haven't seen yet, so thanks for that!
3. It's not a bullet of public perception. It's a bullet of a cancel crowd that can't admit they were wrong, so it's better for them to "confirm their rightness" by killing the innocent and satisfy the actual monster they always were.  
What The Hunt shows (not "proves") is how the majority waits their whole lives in suspension to release their sadism and frustrated self-hatred on those whom society would deem "appropriate targets". Nothing in the actions of the crowd in this movie happens because someone genuinely believed (I'm not even saying had a good reason to) they are doing the right thing and the guy is guilty. They were all just happy to be able to fuck someone's life up without getting any bad consequencies for it, moreover, made feel like "white knights" by the same kind of monsters around them.
4. Firstly, enjoyable write-up, and I love this film. It's a very unsettling work, and I have watched it several times.

## 4. First Cow: Enriching a film by highlighting its context

**Score:** 16
**Body:** In First Cow (2019), Kelly Reichardt does something pretty curious with editing, giving all those slices of the pie that add up around the main thread an unusual narrative force. Call them secondary elements, context, subplots, or simply narrative landscape. All the stuff that enriches a movie, where circumstances and characters outside the protagonists appear to give the story weight, personality, and color --to give it, in the end, a life of its own -- Reichardt invites it to sit at the table and share bread with this pair who team up to give birth to a business as sweet as it is fleeting.

I’m talking about using image and sound in much longer tempos than usual to enrich every sequence. It isn’t just contemplative intent. It’s temporarily taking care of an unknown baby in a tavern full of roughnecks and boors. Handing the closing keys of a scene to a poor guy who has been left without his honeyed bun. Carefully moving through the line of people waiting to buy the market’s triumphant sweet and seeing the convergence of races, cultures, and ways of life, all gathered together for such a delightful purpose. Juicy... purely pleasurable.

On top of that, this slowed-down tempo fits really well with the intention of distorting explicit violence by placing parallels in one single place, but across two different eras. A dog digging and eagerly unearthing bones in the woods connects directly with a final shot that lets us assume the fate of two partners who proved to be much more than that, leaving behind the stamped legacy of both bodies lying togehter, side by side, for eternity.

I recently watched The mastermind, and it feels like a mark of Kelly’s style to use the resources that complement the core of her films in this way. In First Cow, I loved it because I think it’s done very subtly and everything fits. But that personal taste also runs the risk, when taken too far, of sending the central line adrift, with so much weight placed on secondary parts that the protagonist ends up shipwrecked, dragging the viewer along with him in a rotten wooden boat with limited food. Basically, you can end up feeling drowsy from the gum being stretched too much. In The Mastermind, I felt that in moments like the whole car ride with those pseudo-mobsters who show up with very little to offer and leave with even less. And I’m left with almost empty hands.

All of this was an example of how what Reichardt achieves in First Cow is not as easy as it looks, nor is it just a matter of leaving everything at the mercy of a taste for slow cinema or the viewer’s patience.

\*

NOTE:  I want to clarify that I wrote this entirely myself as a personal reflection in spanish, and I simply used deepl to translate certain words or expressions into english so I could post it here, since I’m not a native english speaker and didn’t want the personal touch and warmth with which I wrote it to get lost in a completely manual translation which, based on past experience, tends to make the text a bit more colloquial in some parts and loses what I was talking about. It’s not like I’m trying to make it sound like a thesis hahshah. I like it to sound natural but I feel bad that what I was talking about gets lost in some way.

I'm starting to post in english communities and subreddits after years writing in spanish and for myself and the people I know close. So I will put this note at the end of most of the posts I create here where I write my reflections cause some people hast told me in comments that my texts were written by AI --as I'm used and I like to write in this way, with em dashes, for example-- and is such a pity that all the time and effort one put into writing and looking for what people around the world think goes to gets lost because of a suspicion that I fully understand, of course, because of the times we live in. And I’m aware that many people use AI for these things just to get some interaction. That’s not my case. To me, it sounds absurd to write or rewrite --not even publishing-- something that didn’t come from you. It doesn’t help you to get to know yourself and draw insights from what you see, hear, or read, nor does it help you learn from others. Besides being rather sad and pathetic. It’s a rather paradoxical waste of time, since writing on your own takes infinitely longer. But I just don’t see the point.

And you can elaborate much further. The gentleness with which one milks her reflects a much more empathetic and sustainable philosophy; while his chinese partner remains the more ingenious of the two in the partnership they form , essentially the brain behind the business. he continues to view the cow as a limited resource and is already thinking of another business model for when the current one runs dry. It’s somewhat like countries in the Middle East where oil brought immense wealth and where, aware that scarcity will one day come, they hedge their bets by investing in other structures and models that will keep them in power forever, even going so far as to create entirely artificial sectors where such a thing was once unthinkable.

2. It would be interesting to hear what you think about the film that comes between First Cow and The Mastermind, Showing Up. 

First Cow and The Mastermind have a narrative engine in their enterprise-adventure component. After the three midlength chapters of Certain Women, and the innovative period film First Cow, Reichardt, with Showing Up, comes back to a contemporary character study with a kind of slight, anecdotal story like from Certain Women, but expanded to a feature length. The question for Showing Up is: are the problems and dramas which occur during this week-in-the-life of its protagonist interesting and evocative enough to make a satisfactory feature film, or not?

According to my personal taste, yes, Showing Up is a good film. But it did prompt some reviewers to have the kind of reaction which you are describing having to parts of The Mastermind. The Mastermind itself also prompted similar responses from reviewers.

Reviewer Roger Moore on Showing Up: “
pointilistically-observed minutia in which the minutia’s the point.”

Helen O’Hara, Empire Magazine, on The Mastermind: “
A crime thriller with no interest in thrills and not much in crime, this is an at times frustrating character study of a guy who can’t get out of his own way.”

Your point about, yes Kelly Reichardt has a style, and to those of us familiar with it, and who like it (Reichardt’s slow cinema), it might be interesting enough to simply observe her newest iteration of her style — but not quite. It has to be calibrated with a sufficiency of interesting elements to really engage the viewer. Or, it has to all work for the viewer — some particular detail that one finds incongruent or disruptive can have an effect of being frustrating / irritating / disappointing perhaps even disproportionately so. (Like the screen time of the gangsters in The Mastermind is short, but if they kind of disrupt your sense of the film in some way, this element can be said to have had a disproportionately frustrating effect.)

I still rate Showing Up and The Mastermind positively, but o also think that their content is only just sufficient enough to be properly interesting. Sorry I have no more to say about First Cow. It has been too long since I watched it.

What's special about *The Mastermind* to me is watching a no-life slip into the background of real history. His story, his life, is ultimately of no consequence, ostensibly foregrounded by the film.

Both are aimless individuals who end up seeing a business opportunity by appropriating someone else’s resources (whether it’s a cow’s milk or illustrations from a museum). Then, of course, the characters are very different. One is an honest man and the other a bumbling slacker, and both maintain that attitude until the end, which is reflected in their relationship with their surroundings and with the other characters around them.

## 5. Has anyone else found that genre cinema changes their relationship with 'the canon?'

**Score:** 13
**Body:** I spent most of my film-watching life prioritising 'the canon,' but somewhere in my late 30s I fell headlong into genre films, exploitation cinema, midnight movies, and so on.

I'd kind of been avoiding that side of cinema. Later, I learned I'm AuDHD, and I realised my black and white thinking had a lot to do with how I categorised films as either good" or bad. And, surprise surprise, as soon as I started digging into those areas, I loved what I was watching, and none of it felt lesser. If anything, it made everything else make more sense.

I started to see the lineage, the fusion and the influences running in both direction. One feeding the other and back again.

Since then, I've been building my own viewing strand, exploring the corners of cinema while sprinkling in old favourites, and it's been a joy from beginning to end.

I'd love to hear whether others have had a similar experience, or if you have recommendations that sit in that space between arthouse and genre.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. My relationship to the canon is that it's a ridiculous thing to try make exist and any insistence on it is a waste of time. Jonathan Rosenbuam made the most compelling argument to me on this years ago, THE canon is inherently reductive and reactionary, while a personal canon made by each cinephile from their personalities, perspectives and politics is far more condusive to the spreading of and democratization of art. Anything resembling a general canon is only useful as training wheels for budding cinephiles (Roger Ebert great movies for example) and should be outgrown with time.
  - I *c*ompletely agree, and Rosenbaum is someone I turn to a lot on exactly this. I've come to see the 'canon' as less as a destination and more as building blocks, to say it's useful for understanding the wider world of cinema but not something to stay inside forever.

The strand of films I watch is basically that idea put into practice. Some films in the strand are canon adjacent reference points, used deliberately to build toward something more personal. Others sit way outside it entirely. The whole thing is an attempt at building a personal canon rather than deferring to an inherited one. 
  - This comment raises three questions for me, and while they'll probably get many downvotes they're genuine and I don't know the answers. The questions are:

1 What does democratised art look like? 2 How does the rejection of a canon democratise art? 3 Why do we want art to be democratised? 

(Maybe question 1 and 3 are the same, not sure.)
2. I've found that film's relationship with genre is a lot healthier than literatures. Noir is respected, and is basically trashy crime novels adapted to the screen with elevated cinematography. Science fiction is much more accepted in the film cannon than in literature, and horror has been a major part of film history since before film had sound.

I think there are a few reasons for film: other artforms have long classical traditions stretching back to antiquity, but film has always been modern; film is incredibly expensive to produce compared to literature, and so has been more shameless about seeking mass commercial appeal; lastly, literature's dependence on metaphor as an artistic device means that writers describimg fantastical things must often sacrifice writerly-ness for clarity, and film does not have this problem. Brandon Sanderson has remarked that fantasy is great because you can make your metaphors literal, but the flipside of that is that if the text isn't grounded in normal reality, it can be genuinely difficult to distinguish metaphor from description. Some authors like Gene Wolfe play this up effectively, but many others just ditch complex metaphor in favor of a flat writing style, for the sake of retaining clarity. Film does not have this problem because it does not rely so much on metaphor: an artsy shot of a spaceship will always scan, clearly, as a spaceship, so filmmakers can explore more fantastical things without sacrificing the more artistic qualities of the craft.
3. I feel a certain tension around this, because I have my own, evolving personal 'canon' I refer to when recommending films/thinking about films etc. It's full of arthouse stuff (many that would be included in the official cannon). But there are genre films I genuinely think are masterful, and yet I don't include them, and I'm not sure why. It just feels wrong to put Die Hard and Wild Strawberries on the same list. But honestly I can't argue that there is less talent and artistry involved in the creation of Die Hard than of Wild Strawberries. They're both masterpieces in my eyes. It's uncomfortable for me because I'm someone who likes things to be organised and labelled.
4. The job of a canon is not to be a definitive list of the movies you're going to like the most. It's not even to be a definitive list of the greatest (or most influential) movies of all time. 

It's a snapshot of a collective consensus at a particular point in time. Depending on how the canon is created, it might be a fairly stable consensus, or it might change more over time. Think the Letterboxd top 500 vs Sight & Sound vs They Shoot Pictures. Those lists are varying degrees of stable, put together by different collections of contributors.

A canon is not a substitute for your personal taste. Our favorite movies are not necessarily going to be the movies we admire the most artistically, or recognize as the most influential, or the most popular. Those are all different questions. 

A canon is useful to get a sense of movies that a lot of people think are worth paying attention to. It's a tool for education. Being familiar with the canon is useful if you're interested in a broad perspective on film history, and far less useful if you just want to watch something tonight that you're going to love. 
5. I would say first of all that I think it’s silly to treat arthouse vs genre as a binary. Especially nowadays, a lot of the canon filmmakers make genre films or at least incorporate genre elements and stylings into their work. The most highbrow arthouse film nominated for Best Picture this year was The Secret Agent, a political thriller with a stop motion monster sequence. Genre filmmaking is basically the only way any new talent in America can break in to Hollywood anymore because the budgets are kept low and Americans no longer pay money to watch dramas in theaters. What filmmakers from the 2010s will be remembered in 50 years? Christopher Nolan, Guillermo Del Toro, Jordan Peele, Bong Joon Ho? There’s an awful lot of genre films between those people. Using “genre film” as a derisive thing feels like condescending to an actor for doing TV: the label existed for a reason in 1986 but in 2026 it’s a very silly line to draw because that’s not how the industry works.

However, and this might not win me a ton of brownie points, I have very little time for the “exploitation/midnight movie” end of the genre film spectrum. That stuff appealed to me when I was in school and I’ve felt no desire to throw on Cannibal Ferox or whatever for a rewatch. I think it’s very silly how some of the prestige blu-ray companies release $50 collector’s sets of blatantly shitty horror movies with hours of bonus features. People buy them so I guess they have their fans, that’s cool, but it’s not for me at this stage of my life.
  - Really fair points, and I think you're right that the binary has eroded a lot since the 80s, Bong Joon Ho, Peele, Del Toro are proof of that.

On the exploitation/midnight movie thing though — I'd push back slightly on lumping all of it into the same bucket. I'm not interested in ironic "so bad it's good" appreciation at all, and I think that's a different thing entirely from the films I watch.  And thinkning on that, that could be where my aversion stems from, I had friends that loved that kind of film I could never see the appeal.   
  
The films I like to watch are there because of what they connect to, not because they're fun to laugh at. More in the spirit of something like Moviedrome, using a 'midnight movie'  - which is a broad specturm - to explain the wider cinematic world it came from, what it was trying to say, who it influenced and who influenced it. Context over camp.

Sounds like exploitation just isn't for you anymore though, which is completely fair. Tastes shift.
